blogIndex.card_wildflowers_excerpt: The bay bursts into colour between March and May. Orchids, iris, and rockrose line the roads from Budva to Risan and up toward the Lovcen foothills.

blogHiddenChurches.title: Secret Churches and Chapels of Budva's Old Town
blogHiddenChurches.subtitle: Beyond the cathedral, the medieval lanes conceal a dozen smaller sanctuaries, some unlocked, some behind unmarked doors, all worth finding.
blogHiddenChurches.description: A walking guide to the hidden churches and chapels inside Budva's UNESCO Old Town. Locations, opening hours, and the stories behind each sacred space.
blogHiddenChurches.h2Intro: A Town Built on Faith and Stone
blogHiddenChurches.introP1: Budva's Old Town measures barely 600 metres from gate to gate, yet it holds more than twenty churches and chapels within its walls. Visitors flock to the Cathedral of St Tryphon, photograph its twin towers, and move on. Meanwhile, a dozen smaller sanctuaries sit unlocked in quiet lanes, their frescoed interiors seen by almost nobody.
blogHiddenChurches.introP2: This guide covers six of the most rewarding. All are within the Old Town walls, all are free to enter (donations welcomed), and none appear on the standard cruise-ship walking tour.
blogHiddenChurches.stLukeP1: Built in 1195, St Luke's sits on the small piazza of the same name. For centuries it served both Orthodox and Catholic congregations simultaneously, a dual-altar arrangement found almost nowhere else in Europe. The interior is dim, the iconostasis richly painted, and the worn stone floor carries the imprint of eight hundred years of worshippers.
blogHiddenChurches.stLukeP2: Step inside and allow your eyes to adjust. The ceiling is low, the walls close, and the silence is immediate. The Orthodox iconostasis dates from the 17th century and features saints rendered in gold leaf against dark backgrounds. In the floor beneath your feet, glass panels reveal layers of previous flooring, the earliest from the 12th century. The church is open daily from approximately 08:00 to 19:00 in summer, with shorter hours in winter. No entry fee is charged, but a donation box sits near the door.
blogHiddenChurches.altChapel: Interior of a small stone chapel in Budva Old Town
blogHiddenChurches.h2StMary: St Mary's Collegiate Church (Sveta Marija Koledjata)
blogHiddenChurches.stMaryP1: Positioned near the beginning of the fortress trail, St Mary's is where most locals once worshipped daily. The 13th-century building is modest from the outside but the carved stone doorway repays close examination. Today it serves as the ticket point for the fortress climb, which means most visitors walk straight past the interior without pausing.
blogHiddenChurches.stMaryP2: The church interior was badly damaged in the 1979 earthquake and has been only partially restored. What remains is atmospheric rather than polished, exposed stone walls, a simple altar, and fragments of medieval carving set into the side walls. A small collection of photographs near the entrance documents the earthquake damage and the ongoing restoration work. It is a sober reminder that these buildings have survived not through preservation but through persistence.
blogHiddenChurches.stAnnaP1: Tucked into a blind alley off the lane between Trg od Brasna and the North Gate, St Anna's chapel is easy to miss entirely. The door is usually ajar during daylight hours. Inside: a single small room with a stone altar, a faded fresco above, and the profound quiet that comes from being invisible to passing foot traffic.
blogHiddenChurches.stAnnaP2: The fresco above the altar is believed to date from the 15th century, though centuries of candle smoke have obscured much of the detail. On bright mornings, sunlight enters through a narrow side window and catches the painted surface in a way that reveals colours otherwise invisible, blues and reds emerging from the soot. The alley outside often has cats sleeping on the warm stones, which is how several visitors have stumbled upon the chapel accidentally while following a cat.
blogHiddenChurches.stMichaelP1: Near the River Gate entrance, St Michael's blends Romanesque and Gothic elements in a building that has survived earthquakes, fires, and centuries of neglect. The bell tower leans noticeably. A small collection of religious art is displayed inside during summer months.
blogHiddenChurches.stMichaelP2: The church sits on a small raised platform above the lane, giving it a slightly elevated position that catches the morning light beautifully. The Romanesque arched windows on the south wall are original 12th-century work, while the Gothic pointed arch of the main doorway was added during 14th-century renovations. Inside, look for the carved stone baptismal font near the entrance, it dates from the 13th century and features crude but expressive depictions of fish and vines. The lean of the bell tower is a consequence of the 1667 earthquake; remarkably, it has not worsened in the centuries since.
blogHiddenChurches.stPaulP: This tiny oratory sits above a confraternity meeting room accessible through a courtyard near the main square. The upstairs chapel contains painted panels from the 17th century and a view through a narrow window directly onto the cathedral square, a vantage point the confraternity used to observe public events without being seen. For more on exploring Budva on foot, read our guide to the
blogHiddenChurches.h2StNicholas: The Church of St Nicholas (Sveti Nikola)
blogHiddenChurches.stNicholasP1: The largest Orthodox church in the Old Town, St Nicholas dominates the piazza of St Luke with its imposing facade. Built in 1909 on the site of an earlier church, it is considerably newer than its neighbours but architecturally striking. Two bell towers frame the entrance, and the interior is decorated with large-scale icons and a richly carved iconostasis.
blogHiddenChurches.stNicholasP2: While not exactly hidden, it is visible from across the square, St Nicholas is often overlooked because visitors assume it is a museum or civic building rather than an active church. Services are held regularly, and the interior is open to visitors between services. The contrast between St Nicholas and St Luke's, which face each other across the piazza, encapsulates eight centuries of architectural change in a single glance.
blogHiddenChurches.walkingP1: Start at the Sea Gate and turn right toward St Luke's Square. Visit St Luke's, then continue north through the back lanes to St Anna's chapel. Double back toward the cathedral, pause at St Mary's near the fortress steps, and finish at St Michael's by the River Gate. The entire loop takes forty minutes at a slow pace with time to sit inside each chapel.
blogHiddenChurches.walkingP2: For the extended route, add St Nicholas on St Luke's Square and the Oratory of St Paul near the cathedral. This version takes about an hour and covers virtually every significant religious building within the walls. Carry a small torch for the darker interiors, phone lights work but a proper torch reveals fresco details that overhead lighting misses.
blogHiddenChurches.parkingP: The Old Town is entirely pedestrianised. Park at the River Gate lot (1.50 euros per hour, capacity roughly 150 cars) or along the Bečići strip north of the walls (free outside peak season). On cruise-ship days, arrive before 08:00 for the River Gate lot, or park in Bečići and walk 10 minutes along the waterfront. All churches are within the walls and accessible on foot within minutes of each other.
blogHiddenChurches.tip1Val: Shoulders and knees should be covered in all churches. Carry a light scarf in summer.
blogHiddenChurches.tip2Val: Permitted without flash in most chapels. Ask before photographing icons or altarpieces.
blogHiddenChurches.tip3Val: Early morning before 09:00 or late afternoon after 16:00, when the lanes are quietest and the light through doorways is warmest.
blogHiddenChurches.tip4Val: A euro or two in the collection box helps maintain these buildings. Many have no formal funding.
blogHiddenChurches.tip5Val: Local guides offer Old Town walking tours that include several of these churches. Ask at the tourist information office near the Sea Gate for schedules and prices.

blogVrmacRidgeTrail.title: Driving and Hiking the Vrmac Ridge Between Budva and Tivat Airport
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.subtitle: The forested ridge separating the inner bay from the outer bay, park at the trailhead and walk to the Austro-Hungarian fortress at the summit.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.description: Hike the Vrmac Ridge between Budva and Tivat Airport. Trailhead parking, route options, fortress ruins, and panoramic Budva Riviera views.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.introP1: The Vrmac peninsula is the mountainous finger of land that separates the inner Budva Riviera from the outer bay near Tivat Airport. From below, it appears as a dark forested wall. From the top, it reveals panoramic views in every direction: Budva's fortress to the east, Porto Montenegro to the west, Rafailovići across the water to the north, and the open Adriatic beyond the Verige strait to the south.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.introP2: The ridge trail is Budva's best-kept hiking secret, far less crowded than the fortress climb, with better views and more shade. A rental car makes it accessible: the trailhead is a 10-minute drive from Budva Old Town.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.trailheadP1: From Budva, drive south toward Tivat Airport. After the Vrmac tunnel entrance (which you do not take), a small road branches left and climbs steeply through the hamlet of Prcanj. Follow signs for Vrmac or the Austrian fortress. The trailhead has space for about eight cars on a gravel pull-off. Arrive before 09:00 in summer to secure a spot.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.trailheadP2: The approach road from Prcanj is narrow and steep, with a few tight bends between stone walls. Any car can handle it in dry conditions, but an SUV provides more ground clearance on the final unpaved section. If the trailhead lot is full, there is space for two or three more cars 200 metres back down the road on a widened verge.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.altRidge: View from the Vrmac ridge trail looking down on both sides of Budva Riviera
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.routeP1: The main path follows the spine of the ridge through dense Mediterranean forest, holm oak, laurel, and wild herbs that scent the air. The surface is a mix of compacted earth and exposed rock. It is not technically difficult, but the elevation gain is steady and some sections are steep.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.routeP2: After approximately 45 minutes of walking, you reach the ruins of Fort Vrmac, an Austro-Hungarian military installation built in the 1860s to control the Riviera entrance. The fort is partially ruined but explorable. The rooftop (accessible with care) provides the 360-degree panorama that makes the effort worthwhile.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.routeP3: The trail continues beyond the fort along the ridge toward Tivat Airport for another 30 to 40 minutes. This extended section is less travelled and passes through thicker forest before emerging at a second, smaller fortification. Most hikers turn back at the main fort, but the extension is worthwhile if you have the energy, the solitude increases dramatically past the first ruin.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.fortP1: Fort Vrmac was part of a chain of defensive positions the Austrian Empire built around the Budva Riviera in the 19th century. It was designed to house artillery commanding the Verige strait, the narrow passage connecting the inner and outer bay. Today the walls stand, the underground chambers are dark and damp, and the rooftop offers what might be the single best viewpoint on the entire bay.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.fortP2: Explore the fort at your own pace, but exercise caution. The underground chambers are unlit and the floors uneven. Bring a head torch if you want to explore them properly. The rooftop is accessed by a crumbling external staircase, sturdy shoes are essential. From the top, you can identify nearly every landmark on the Riviera: Budva's walls, Rafailovići's palazzi, the two islands, Porto Montenegro's masts, and the Verige narrows where the inner and outer bays connect.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.wildlifeP: The Vrmac ridge is a surprisingly rich habitat. In spring, wild orchids and cyclamen grow beside the trail. Year-round, you may spot Hermann's tortoises crossing the path, they are common on the ridge and entirely harmless. Birdsong fills the forest canopy: warblers, woodpeckers, and the distinctive call of the Eurasian jay. In autumn, the holm oaks drop acorns that crunch underfoot, and the laurel trees fill the air with their distinctive fragrance.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip1Val: 90 minutes to 2 hours round trip from the trailhead to the fort and back.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip2Val: Carry at least 1 litre per person. There is no water source on the trail.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip3Val: Trail shoes or sturdy trainers. The rocky sections are uneven.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip4Val: Most of the trail is forested, making it more comfortable than the exposed fortress climb in Budva.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip5Val: Walkable year-round. Spring (April-May) is ideal, wildflowers, mild temperatures, and clear air.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.tip6Val: The fort rooftop is fully exposed. Sunscreen and a hat are essential in summer, even if the trail below was shaded.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.combineP1: After the hike, drive through the Vrmac tunnel to Tivat Airport for lunch at Porto Montenegro, or return to Budva and cool off at one of the Riviera's swimming spots. Read our guide to the
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.combineLink: best swimming spots around the Riviera
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.combineP2: for nine options within 30 minutes of Budva.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.seasonalP1: Spring (April to May) is the finest season for the Vrmac ridge. Wildflowers carpet the clearings, the air is cool enough for comfortable hiking, and visibility across the Riviera is at its clearest. The trail surface is dry by mid-April after the winter rains, and the forest canopy provides dappled shade.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.seasonalP2: Summer brings heat. By mid-morning in July, temperatures on the exposed fort rooftop can exceed 35 degrees Celsius. Start early, aim to be on the trail by 07:30, and carry extra water. The forested sections remain tolerable, but the open areas around the fort are punishing in the midday sun.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.seasonalP3: Autumn (September to October) is a close second to spring. The summer crowds have left, the Riviera water below is still warm enough for a post-hike swim, and the holm oaks begin to drop acorns that crunch underfoot. Winter hiking is possible but the trail can be muddy after rain, and the exposed sections are cold and windswept.
blogVrmacRidgeTrail.parkingP: The trailhead parking is free but limited to roughly eight vehicles. In peak summer (July and August), spaces fill by 09:00. Alternatives: park in Prcanj village and walk up the access road (adds 20 minutes each way), or arrange a drop-off with your accommodation. The access road is not suitable for large vehicles or camper vans.

blogBudvaFoodMarkets.title: Market Day in Budva: Where Locals Buy Fish, Cheese, and Wine
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.subtitle: A morning guide to the Green Market, waterfront fish stalls, and the producers who supply Budva's kitchens.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.description: Guide to Budva's food markets. Where to find fresh fish, Njeguski cheese, Crmnica wine, and seasonal produce at the Green Market and bay-road stalls.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.introP1: Every morning except Sunday, the Green Market outside Budva's Old Town fills with produce from the surrounding hills and bay. Farmers from the Njeguski highlands bring smoked ham and young cheese. Fishermen from Rafailovići and Bečići lay out the morning catch on ice. Wine producers from the Crmnica valley around Lake Skadar pour samples from unmarked bottles.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.introP2: For visitors with a rental car, the market is the starting point for a food-focused day that takes in roadside producers along the Riviera road, a village cheese maker in the Lovcen foothills, and a winery tasting south of the lake.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.greenMarketP1: Located just outside the Old Town walls near the River Gate car park, the market occupies a covered hall and an overflow of open-air stalls along the adjacent lane. Seasonal fruit and vegetables dominate: tomatoes, peppers, figs, and pomegranates in late summer; citrus and hardy greens in winter. Honey vendors sell chestnut, wildflower, and sage varieties, taste before you buy, as quality varies.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.greenMarketP2: The covered hall is the heart of the market. Permanent stalls along the walls sell dried herbs, olive oil in reused water bottles, pickled vegetables, and bags of ground coffee. The central aisle fills with temporary tables where farmers from the surrounding villages spread their goods each morning. By 11:00, the best produce is gone and stallholders begin packing up. Saturday morning has the fullest selection, with producers making the trip from more distant villages who skip the weekday markets.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.altMarket: Fresh produce stalls at Budva's Green Market
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.fishP1: Fresh bay fish appears on trestle tables along the waterfront near the market from around 07:00. Sardines, anchovies, sea bream, and squid are the staples. Arrive early, the best fish goes to restaurant owners who shop before tourists wake. If you are staying in accommodation with a kitchen, a kilo of sardines costs a few euros and grills beautifully on a simple barbecue.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.fishP2: The fishmongers here are the same families who have worked the Riviera for generations. They will tell you what is freshest, recommend cooking methods, and occasionally throw in a handful of herbs from their garden. Ask for orada (sea bream) if you want a whole grilled fish, a kilo runs about 12 to 15 euros. For something simpler, sardines at 3 to 4 euros per kilo are the everyday staple of bay cooking.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.cheeseP1: The mountain village of Njeguski, 30 minutes above Budva on the Lovcen road, produces Montenegro's most celebrated cheese, a semi-hard sheep's milk wheel with a distinctive smoky rind. The accompanying prsut (dry-cured ham) is air-dried in the mountain winds. Both are available at the Green Market, but for the freshest supply and the experience of buying directly from a farmhouse kitchen, drive up to Njeguski village itself.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.cheeseP2: At the market, look for the stalls with whole cheese wheels on display, these are direct from producers rather than wholesale resellers. A kilogram of njeguski sir costs approximately 10 to 14 euros at the market, slightly less if you buy directly in the village. The ham is sold sliced or as a whole leg. For a picnic on the Riviera road, buy 200 grams of each (about 5 euros total) along with fresh bread from the bakery on Ulica od Gradskih Bastiona, just inside the River Gate.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.wineP1: Montenegro's wine region sits around Lake Skadar, about 90 minutes from Budva. The Plantaze state winery produces Vranac (a bold red) and Krstac (a crisp white) in quantity, but the smaller family vineyards along the Crmnica valley offer tastings and bottle sales with considerably more charm. A rental car turns this into an afternoon excursion that pairs naturally with a morning at the Budva market.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.wineP2: At the Green Market, several wine sellers pour samples from unmarked bottles at a small table beside the covered hall. These are typically homemade wines, variable in quality but always interesting. If you find one you like, buy a few bottles. Prices are remarkably low: 3 to 5 euros for a litre bottle of decent table wine. For something more refined, the Plantaze shop in Podgorica sells their full range at cellar-door prices.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.oliveOilP: The bay road between Budva and Rafailovići passes through olive groves that have produced oil for centuries. Several families sell directly from their homes, look for hand-painted signs reading maslinovo ulje (olive oil) along the roadside. The oil is cold-pressed, peppery, and intensely green in early season (November harvest). A litre costs 8 to 12 euros, and the quality rivals anything from Italy or Greece. Bring an empty bottle or buy one of the reused mineral water bottles they fill on the spot.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.honeyP: Montenegro produces exceptional honey, and the market is the best place to taste it. Three varieties dominate: sage honey (light, floral, and prized above all others), chestnut honey (dark, intense, and slightly bitter), and wildflower honey (variable depending on the hillside and the season). Taste before you buy, quality varies between producers, and the sage honey from the Lovcen foothills is noticeably different from the coastal wildflower variety. A 500-gram jar costs 5 to 8 euros. The honey vendors occupy the corner of the covered hall nearest the Old Town walls.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.h2WhereToEat: Where to Eat After the Market
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.whereToEatP: After shopping, walk through the River Gate into the Old Town for coffee and breakfast. Forza on the main piazza serves strong espresso and fresh pastries from 07:30. For a more substantial market breakfast, buy fresh bread from the bakery on Ulica od Gradskih Bastiona, add some cheese and ham from the market, and eat on the stone benches beside the River Gate with the Riviera as your backdrop. This is how Budva locals start their Saturday.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.routeMorningVal: Park at the River Gate lot. Walk to the fish stalls for the freshest selection.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.routeMidVal: Browse the Green Market. Sample honey, buy cheese, pick up fruit for the car.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.routeLateVal: Drive up the Lovcen road to Njeguski village. Buy ham and cheese directly from a farmhouse.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.routeNoonVal: Return to Budva for coffee in the Old Town, or continue over the mountain toward Cetinje.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.routeAfternoonVal: If continuing to the Crmnica valley, follow the road through Cetinje to Virpazar and turn south toward the family vineyards. Allow 2 hours for the round trip from Cetinje.
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.alsoP: If you are interested in local food culture, our guide to
blogBudvaFoodMarkets.alsoLink: Bečići's waterfront restaurants and wine bars

blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.title: Best Swimming Spots Around the Budva Riviera
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.subtitle: From pebble beaches beneath fig trees to concrete platforms with ladders into deep blue water, nine places to swim that most visitors never find.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.description: Nine swimming spots around the Budva Riviera you can drive to. Hidden beaches, concrete platforms, and quiet family coves.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.introP1: The Budva Riviera is not a beach destination in the traditional sense. There are no wide sandy stretches lapped by gentle waves. Instead, the Riviera offers something different: crystal-clear water beneath towering mountains, accessed from stone jetties, pebble coves, and concrete platforms with ladders bolted to the rock. The swimming is excellent, the water is clean, calm, and deep enough to dive into from the shore in many places.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.introP2: A rental car is essential for reaching most of these spots. Several are nothing more than a widened section of the Riviera road with a ladder descending into the water. Others are proper beaches with facilities, but set back from the main towns where buses do not run.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.introP3: Water temperature in the Riviera ranges from about 14 degrees Celsius in January to a comfortable 25 to 27 degrees in July and August. The inner bay warms slightly faster than the outer sections near the Verige strait, where tidal exchange with the open Adriatic keeps temperatures a degree or two cooler. Visibility is typically excellent, 5 to 10 metres on a calm day.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot1P1: Between Budva and Rafailovići, the small settlement of Orahovac has a pebble beach backed by a handful of trees. The water is deep and remarkably clear. A waterfront restaurant serves grilled fish and cold beer. Park on the roadside or in the small lot behind the settlement. Ten minutes from Budva, yet it feels like a different pace entirely.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot1P2: The beach faces west, which means it gets afternoon and evening sun, making it an ideal late-day swim spot. A stone jetty extends into the water at the southern end, providing a platform for jumping and easy ladder access. The restaurant, Konoba Orahovac, is one of the best-value seafood spots on the Riviera road, grilled fish with salad and wine for under 15 euros.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.altSwim: Clear water and rocky shoreline at a swimming spot on the Budva Riviera
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot2P: A rocky promontory jutting into the Riviera about 5 minutes north of Budva. Concrete platforms with metal ladders provide access to deep, clean water. Popular with locals who swim here after work. No sand, no facilities, just water and mountains.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot3P: At the southern end of Rafailovići's waterfront, a small concrete bathing area faces directly across to the island of Our Lady of the Rocks. The water is deep and the view while floating on your back, islands, mountains, baroque palaces, is hard to beat anywhere in Europe.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot4P: A 20-minute drive from Tivat Airport through the Lustica peninsula brings you to Plavi Horizonti, the Riviera area's only proper sandy beach. Shallow, warm water makes it suitable for families with small children. A beachside bar rents loungers and umbrellas in season.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot5P: The tiny village of Stoliv, on the Riviera's western shore between Budva and Tivat Airport, has stone steps leading directly from the road into the water. Virtually no tourists stop here. The water is deep, sheltered, and surrounded on three sides by mountains.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot6P1: At the head of the Riviera's north-western arm, Morinj sits where a cold freshwater spring meets the sea. The water temperature drops noticeably, refreshing on a hot day, startling if you are not expecting it. A couple of simple restaurants nearby make it a good lunch-and-swim combination.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot6P2: Catovica Mlini, the restaurant beside the spring outlet, is worth the trip regardless of swimming. Set beneath ancient plane trees with a millstream running through the terrace, it serves traditional Montenegrin dishes and fresh trout from the spring. It has been operating since the 18th century and the atmosphere is extraordinary. Reserve ahead in summer.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot7P: The village of Prcanj, between Budva and Tivat Airport, has several unmarked swimming ladders along its waterfront promenade. The massive unfinished cathedral of the Birth of Our Lady provides an unusual architectural backdrop for a morning swim.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot8P: Further along the Lustica peninsula from Blue Horizons, Zanjice is a pebbly beach at the peninsula's tip. Boats to the Blue Cave depart from here. The beach itself is pleasant and less developed than Plavi Horizonti.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.spot9P: Risan's long, quiet waterfront has several points where concrete steps lead into the Riviera. Combine a morning swim with a visit to the Roman mosaics, which are a two-minute walk inland. The water here is the stillest in the entire bay, the surrounding mountains block most wind.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.tip1Val: Essential at rocky and pebbly spots. The stone can be sharp and sea urchins inhabit some areas.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.tip2Val: Many spots use metal ladders bolted to concrete. They are sturdy but can be slippery when wet.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.tip3Val: Bay water is often deep right at the shore. Do not assume shallow entry, check before jumping.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.tip4Val: Water temperatures are comfortable from June through September. May and October are swimmable but cooler.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.tip5Val: Occasional in late summer, particularly near the Verige strait where currents bring them from the open sea. They are rarely dangerous but can sting. Ask locals about current conditions before swimming.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.fullDayP: Start at Markov Rt near Bečići for an early-morning swim (08:00). Drive to Orahovac for a mid-morning dip and fish lunch at the waterfront restaurant. Continue past Rafailovići to Risan for a quick swim and the Roman mosaics. Loop back to Stoliv on the western shore for a late-afternoon swim in the quietest water on the Riviera. Return to Budva via the Vrmac tunnel. Total driving time: about 90 minutes across the whole day. Total swimming spots: four, each with a different character.
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.driveP: All nine spots are accessible by car from Budva in under 30 minutes. The bay road connects most of them in a single loop. Pack towels, water shoes, and a cooler bag, and make a day of it. If the Vrmac ridge hike leaves you overheated, our guide covers
blogBayOfBudvaSwimming.driveLink: the ridge trail between Budva and Tivat Airport

blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.title: Budva to Cetinje via the Old Mountain Road
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.subtitle: Skip the highway and take the original mountain road through Njeguski village and over the Lovcen shoulder, smoked ham, monastery relics, and the former royal capital.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.description: Drive the old mountain road from Budva to Cetinje via Njeguski village. Ham tasting, monasteries, and the royal capital.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.introP1: Most drivers heading from Budva to Cetinje take the modern highway via Budva, faster, wider, and entirely forgettable. The original mountain road, by contrast, climbs directly out of the Riviera on a series of switchbacks, passes through the highland hamlet of Njeguski, crosses the Lovcen shoulder, and descends into Cetinje through rolling meadows and stone farmland.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.introP2: The drive takes about 90 minutes including stops, compared to roughly an hour on the highway. The extra thirty minutes buy you smoked ham at a farmhouse door, views that extend to the Italian coast on clear days, and a sense of how Montenegro's old royal capital connected to the sea before tunnels and motorways existed.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.ascentP1: The road begins at the back of Budva, climbing steeply through residential streets before the switchbacks begin. The first section shares the route with the famous Lovcen serpentine, 25 hairpin bends numbered on stone markers. At bend 17, pull over at the marked viewpoint for the classic aerial photograph of Budva and the Riviera below. By bend 25, you have left the coast behind entirely.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.ascentP2: The gradient is significant but the road surface is good. Use second gear for most of the climb and stay aware of downhill traffic, tour coaches occasionally descend this road, and passing is tight on the narrower sections. Each numbered bend has a small widened area on the outside edge that serves as both viewpoint and passing place. The ascent from sea level to the Njeguski plateau takes roughly 30 minutes of continuous driving.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.altMountain: Mountain road winding through the Lovcen foothills above Budva bay
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.h2Njeguski: Njeguski: The Ham and Cheese Village
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.njeguskiP1: Just past the final hairpin, the road levels out and enters the highland village of Njeguski. This is where Montenegro's most famous foods originate: njeguski prsut (dry-cured ham smoked in mountain air) and njeguski sir (a semi-hard sheep's cheese with a distinctive character). Several farmhouses along the road sell both directly, look for handwritten signs. Stop, taste, buy. The ham is among the finest cured meats in the Balkans.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.njeguskiP2: The Petrovic family homestead, about 500 metres past the village centre on the left side, is a reliable stop. They cure their own ham in a stone smokehouse behind the main building and age their cheese in a cellar. A tasting plate of ham, cheese, olives, and homemade bread costs 5 euros, and you can buy vacuum-packed portions to take away. The family also makes a clear grape brandy (lozovaca) that they pour generously for visitors, if you are driving, decline politely and buy a bottle for later instead.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.descentP1: From Njeguski, the road crosses the Lovcen ridge and begins a gentler descent toward the Cetinje plateau. The landscape shifts from bare karst rock to green meadows dotted with grazing sheep. The former royal capital appears in a valley surrounded by low mountains, a modest, dignified town that was once the centre of Montenegrin independence.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.descentP2: The descent is considerably easier than the ascent. The road widens, the gradients lessen, and the views open up across the plateau. In spring, the meadows are covered in wildflowers, buttercups, clovers, and the occasional wild orchid. Sheep grazing beside the road are common and generally unbothered by passing cars, though a lamb occasionally decides to cross at the worst possible moment. Slow down through the pastoral sections.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.cetinjeP1: Cetinje served as Montenegro's capital from 1482 until 1946. The Cetinje Monastery, rebuilt in 1786, safeguards relics including what is claimed to be the right hand of John the Baptist and a fragment of the True Cross. The former Royal Palace of King Nikola I, now a museum, displays paintings, personal items, and photographs from the brief era of Montenegrin monarchy. Both sit on a tree-lined boulevard that retains a distinctly 19th-century atmosphere.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.cetinjeP2: Beyond the two main attractions, Cetinje rewards an hour of wandering. The tree-lined Glavni Grad pedestrian street is flanked by former embassies, grand buildings from the period when Cetinje served as a European capital, now quietly fading. The National Museum has paintings by Montenegrin artists. And the Biljarda Palace, built in 1838 for Prince-Bishop Njegos, houses a relief map of Montenegro carved in the 19th century that is worth seeing for its sheer ambition.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.whereToEatP: Restoran Belvedere, on the Cetinje-Podgorica road just south of town, serves traditional Montenegrin dishes with views across the surrounding hills. The lamb cooked under the sac is exceptional, slow-roasted with potatoes and herbs for several hours. A full meal costs 10 to 15 euros per person. Alternatively, Kole on the main boulevard serves pizza and grilled meats at lower prices with a pleasant terrace for watching Cetinje life go by.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.returnP1: You have three options: retrace the mountain road (dramatic in evening light), take the modern highway via Budva (faster, less interesting), or continue north through Rijeka Crnojevica and along the shore of Lake Skadar before looping back to Podgorica and the coast. The lake route adds two hours but passes through completely different landscape.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.returnP2: If retracing the mountain road at dusk, the descent back into the Riviera provides the day's final spectacle. As you round the upper switchbacks, the Riviera appears below, dark water reflecting the lights of Budva and Bečići. The last stretch of the descent, with the illuminated fortress walls visible ahead, is one of the most memorable drives in the country.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.tip1Val: Any car handles this road. The switchbacks are easier in a compact with good torque. Automatic transmission reduces fatigue.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.tip2Val: April to October is ideal. Winter may bring snow on the upper sections, check road conditions before departure.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.tip3Val: Fill up in Budva. There are no petrol stations between the Riviera and Cetinje on this route.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.tip4Val: Allow a full morning or afternoon. Three hours minimum including stops at Njeguski and Cetinje.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.tip5Val: The viewpoint at bend 17 is the classic shot. But the views from Njeguski village and the descent to Cetinje are equally dramatic on a clear day.
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.alsoP: If the Njeguski food stop whets your appetite, our guide covers
blogBudvaToCetinjeDrive.alsoLink: Budva's food markets and where locals shop

blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.title: Bečići: The Quiet Waterfront Village Next to Budva
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.subtitle: A 10-minute drive from the Old Town walls, Bečići's stone promenade has palazzo ruins, wine bars, and the Riviera's best sunset views.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.description: Explore Bečići village near Budva Old Town. Waterfront walk, palazzo architecture, restaurants, and local wine bars on the Riviera.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.introP1: Bečići begins where Budva ends. Drive north from the Old Town along the Riviera road for ten minutes and the tourist crowds thin to nothing. The waterfront widens into a stone promenade lined with palazzo facades, some restored, some crumbling photogenically, and a scattering of restaurants and wine bars that Budva residents consider their own territory.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.introP2: The village stretches for about 2 km along the water's edge. There is no centre in the traditional sense, just a continuous ribbon of stone, water, and mountain. Park anywhere along the road (free outside summer peak) and walk.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.palazziP1: Bečići's wealthy sea captains built their homes along this stretch during the Venetian period. The Palazzo Tripkovic, Palazzo Dabinovic, and several others line the promenade with carved doorways, iron balconies, and walled gardens visible through rusted gates. Most are privately owned and not open to visitors, but the exteriors alone tell the story of a settlement that once rivalled Rafailovići in maritime wealth.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.palazziP2: Look for the stone carvings above doorways, many feature ships, anchors, or the Venetian lion, indicating the owner's profession and allegiance. The Palazzo Tripkovic has a particularly fine carved lintel showing a galleon under full sail. Several buildings show earthquake damage from 1979 that has never been fully repaired: cracked walls, missing balconies, and the occasional open room visible through a collapsed ceiling. These ruins are part of Bečići's character rather than a sign of neglect.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.altWaterfront: Stone waterfront promenade in Bečići with palazzo facades and bay views
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.churchesP1: Two churches punctuate the waterfront walk. The Church of St Matthew (Sveti Matej) dates from the 15th century and holds a collection of religious paintings attributed to local and Venetian artists. Further along, the Church of St Eustace (Sveti Eustahije) is the larger of the two, with a bell tower that serves as a landmark for boats approaching from across the Riviera.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.churchesP2: St Eustace is Bečići's most significant building. The treasury contains religious silver and gold pieces gifted by sea captains returning from successful voyages. Opening hours are irregular, try mid-morning on weekdays for the best chance of finding it open. A sacristan sometimes sits outside and will unlock the treasury room for interested visitors. No formal entry fee, but a donation of a few euros is appropriate.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.diningP1: Bečići's restaurants occupy converted ground floors of old stone houses, with tables extending onto the waterfront in summer. The cuisine is the same as Budva, grilled fish, black risotto, local salads, but the atmosphere is noticeably calmer and the prices slightly lower. Several wine bars have opened in recent years, pouring Montenegrin wines by the glass alongside small plates of cheese and cured meats.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.diningP2: Konoba Catovica, roughly midway along the waterfront, is a local favourite for grilled fish and house wine. The stone terrace sits at water level, and on still evenings you can hear the Riviera lapping against the steps. Their grilled bream with blitva costs about 14 euros. Further along toward Ljuta, Restoran Stari Mlini (Old Mills) sits beside an old watermill and offers a more refined menu, try the black risotto with cuttlefish ink. Main courses run 14 to 20 euros. Both restaurants are considerably less crowded than anything inside Budva's walls.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.swimmingP: Several concrete platforms and stone steps along the Bečići waterfront provide access to swimming. The water is deep, clean, and relatively warm from June through September. The platform near the Church of St Matthew is popular with locals in the late afternoon. There is no beach as such, this is ladder-and-platform swimming, but the water quality is excellent and the mountain backdrop makes up for the lack of sand. Bring water shoes for the rocky entry points.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.historyP: Bečići's wealth came from the sea. During the centuries of Venetian rule, its captains sailed trading ships across the Mediterranean and returned with money, goods, and architectural ambitions. By the 18th century, Bečići's fleet numbered over 30 merchant vessels, and the village had its own maritime school and insurance fund. The palazzi along the waterfront were their legacy, grand stone houses built to impress visitors arriving by boat. The maritime tradition is documented in Budva's Maritime Museum, a 20-minute drive away, where Bečići captains feature prominently in the collection.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.seasonalP1: Summer Bečići is the village at its liveliest. Restaurant terraces fill from early evening, swimmers splash off the stone platforms, and the waterfront promenade becomes a passeggiata route for locals escaping the Old Town crowds. July and August bring the warmest water and the longest evenings, but also the heaviest parking demand.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.seasonalP2: Spring and autumn are quieter and in many ways more rewarding. The palazzo facades look their best in the soft light of April, when wisteria and bougainvillea drape the garden walls. October brings golden evening light that sets the stone buildings glowing. In winter, Bečići is almost deserted, a few cafes stay open, the churches are unlocked, and you can walk the entire waterfront without seeing another person. The mountains across the Riviera collect snow, and the contrast of white peaks above dark water is striking.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.sunsetP: Bečići faces west across the Riviera, directly into the sunset. The mountains above Rafailovići and Risan turn orange and gold in the last hour of daylight, reflected in the still water. Any point along the promenade works, but the stretch between the two churches is particularly good, unobstructed views, a bench or low wall to sit on, and the quiet broken only by the occasional boat motor.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingP1: From Budva Old Town, drive north on the Riviera road. Bečići begins almost immediately, the transition is seamless. On-street parking is available throughout the village. In July and August, arrive before 18:00 if you want a restaurant table for sunset.
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingP2: Alternatively, walk from Budva. The promenade from the northern walls to the first palazzi of Bečići is about 800 metres along the waterfront, a flat, easy walk that takes 10 to 12 minutes. This is a good option on cruise-ship days when parking near Budva is impossible. For another bay road walk, see our guide to
blogBečićiWaterfrontWalk.gettingLink: the spring wildflower drives around the Riviera

blogBudvaKayakAndCar.title: Kayaking the Bay: Where to Launch from the Coastal Road
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.subtitle: Where to launch a kayak around Budva Riviera, what to see from the water, and how to combine paddling with a driving day trip.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.description: Budva Riviera kayaking guide. Car-accessible launch points, guided tour options, and how to combine kayaking with a road trip.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.introP1: The Budva Riviera was built for boats. For centuries, the only way to move between the Riviera's towns was by water, roads came later and, in some places, barely at all. Seeing the Riviera from a kayak restores that original perspective: the mountains rise higher, the palazzi of Rafailovići look even more improbable, and the islands are close enough to touch.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.introP2: Combining kayaking with a rental car creates the perfect bay day. Drive to a launch point, paddle for two or three hours, dry off, and continue to the next destination along the coast road. No guided tour can offer this flexibility.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch1P1: Several kayak rental operators set up along the waterfront between the Old Town and Bečići from May to October. Guided group tours depart in the morning and evening, typically paddling to the submarine tunnel at the base of the Vrmac ridge. Self-hire is available for experienced paddlers. Park at the Tabacina lot or along the Bečići road.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch1P2: The morning tour typically departs at 09:00 and lasts 2.5 to 3 hours, paddling along the base of the fortress walls, past the Bečići waterfront, and into the mouth of the old Yugoslav submarine tunnel carved into the Vrmac ridge. The evening tour, departing at 17:00 or 18:00 depending on season, catches the golden hour light on the Riviera, the fortress walls glow amber and the water turns glassy. Both options cost approximately 25 to 35 euros per person including equipment.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.altKayak: Kayakers on the Budva Riviera with mountains rising behind
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch2P1: The small settlement of Orahovac, between Budva and Rafailovići, has a pebbly beach where you can launch directly into the Riviera. From here, it is a manageable paddle across to the island of Our Lady of the Rocks, a perspective few visitors experience. The water is typically calm in the morning, with afternoon winds picking up from the south.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch2P2: The crossing from Orahovac to the island is roughly 800 metres, about 15 minutes of moderate paddling. Landing on the island is permitted at the small jetty on the southern side. Bring euros for the small museum entry fee inside the church. The return paddle can be done via a different route, skirting the shore of St George island (landing not permitted) for a close look at the monastery walls and cypress trees.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch3P1: Rafailovići's waterfront offers the most scenic launch point on the Riviera. Paddle out toward the two islands, St George and Our Lady of the Rocks, with the backdrop of sixteen palazzo facades and the mountain wall behind. Kayak rentals are available from operators near the harbour. Park at the northern end of Rafailovići.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch3P2: From Rafailovići, the paddle to Our Lady of the Rocks takes about 10 minutes. Circumnavigating both islands adds another 20 to 30 minutes depending on your pace and how often you stop to photograph. The water between the islands and the shore is sheltered and typically calm, making this an excellent option for less experienced paddlers. Watch for the occasional taxi boat crossing between Rafailovići and the island, they have right of way.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch4P1: The village of Stoliv on the Riviera's western shore is one of the quietest launch points. The water here is deep and calm, sheltered by the Vrmac ridge. Paddle south toward the Verige strait, where the inner and outer bays meet through a gap barely 300 metres wide. The current through the strait can be noticeable, stay close to the shore if unsure.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.launch4P2: Stoliv is the best option for experienced paddlers who want solitude. There is no kayak rental operation here, you would need to bring your own or hire from Budva and drive it to Stoliv on the car roof (most rental operators offer roof racks for this purpose). The reward is paddling in water that sees almost no boat traffic, with the abandoned village of Gornji Stoliv visible on the cliff face 400 metres above.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.h2WhatToSee: What to See from the Water
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.whatToSeeP1: Kayaking reveals details invisible from the road. The fortress walls of Budva descend directly into the water at several points, from a kayak, you can see the original waterline markings and the moss-covered stone steps that once served as boat landings. Along the Bečići waterfront, the palazzo gardens that are hidden behind high walls from the road are visible from the water side. And the submarine tunnel entrance near the Vrmac ridge, a dark cavern carved into the cliff during the Yugoslav era, is only accessible by water.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.whatToSeeP2: The wildlife perspective is different too. Herons stand motionless on the rocky shore, invisible from the road but startled into flight as a kayak passes. Fish jump near the surface in the early morning, mullet mostly, in silvery schools that scatter when a paddle breaks the surface. In the quieter sections near Stoliv, the water is so clear you can see the seabed at four or five metres, with sea grass waving gently below.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.rentalP: Several operators in Budva rent single and double kayaks by the hour. Expect to pay 15 to 20 euros per hour for a single kayak, 25 to 30 for a double. Guided tours are more expensive (25 to 35 euros per person) but include safety briefing, equipment, and a guide who knows the Riviera's currents and conditions. Most operators provide a waterproof bag, life jacket, and paddle. A few also rent stand-up paddleboards, which work well in the calm morning conditions closer to shore.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.tip1Val: Join a guided tour if you have not kayaked before. The bay is calm but distances can be deceiving.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.tip2Val: Morning is almost always calmer. Afternoon winds from the south can create choppy conditions, especially near the Verige strait.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.tip3Val: Water reflects UV intensely. Wear a hat, apply waterproof sunscreen, and bring a long-sleeve rash guard.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.tip4Val: May through October. July and August are busiest. September offers warm water and fewer boats.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.tip5Val: Bring a waterproof bag for your phone, wallet, and car keys. Kayaks can tip, and the Riviera is too deep to retrieve anything dropped overboard.
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.combineP: After paddling, dry off and drive to one of the Riviera's less-visited swimming spots for a final dip. Our guide to
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.combineLink: the best swimming spots around the Riviera
blogBudvaKayakAndCar.combineP2: covers nine options within 30 minutes of Budva.

blogStolivAbandonedVillage.title: Driving to Gornji Stoliv: Budva's Abandoned Cliff Village
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.subtitle: A narrow road climbs 400 metres above the Riviera to a stone village abandoned after the 1979 earthquake. The views and the silence are extraordinary.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.description: Visit Gornji Stoliv, the abandoned cliff village above Budva bay. Driving directions, ruin walk, and views from 400 m up.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.introP1: Gornji Stoliv (Upper Stoliv) clings to a cliff face 400 metres above the Budva Riviera on the western slope of the Vrmac peninsula. Until the devastating earthquake of April 1979, roughly a hundred families lived here, farming the terraced hillside and walking down to the Riviera for fish and trade. The earthquake damaged the buildings beyond easy repair, and the residents relocated to the waterfront settlement of Donji Stoliv below.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.introP2: Today, Gornji Stoliv is one of the most atmospheric abandoned settlements in the Balkans. Stone houses with collapsed roofs, a church with a surviving bell tower, overgrown garden walls, and a silence broken only by birdsong and the distant sound of boats on the Riviera far below.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.driveP1: From Budva, drive south along the Riviera road toward Tivat Airport. After passing through Prcanj, look for a small road branching right (signposted intermittently for Stoliv). The road climbs steeply through olive groves and becomes progressively narrower. The final section is a single-track lane with no guard rails and occasional loose gravel. A compact car handles it in dry conditions, but an SUV provides more confidence. Park at the end of the road where the asphalt gives way to a footpath.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.driveP2: The drive from the Riviera road to the parking area takes about 15 minutes. The gradient is steep, first gear is necessary on the steepest sections. Two passing places exist on the single-track section: one beside a stone water cistern, another at a hairpin bend. If you meet an oncoming vehicle, one of you must reverse to the nearest passing place. This is not a road to attempt if you are uncomfortable with narrow mountain lanes. Check your mirrors before committing to the single-track section.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.altVillage: Abandoned stone houses at Gornji Stoliv with the Budva Riviera far below
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.exploreP1: From the parking area, a footpath leads through the first cluster of ruined houses. The main lane of the village is still traceable, stone walls define what were once rooms, doorways frame views of the Riviera, and fig trees have colonised former kitchens. The church of Sv. Ilija (St Elijah) stands at the highest point of the settlement, its walls intact but the roof gone. The bell tower is the most photographed structure in the village.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.exploreP2: Allow 30 to 45 minutes to walk through the village and absorb the atmosphere. The views from the upper terraces are staggering, the entire inner bay is visible, from Budva to Rafailovići to the Verige strait.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.exploreP3: Wander beyond the main cluster and you find agricultural terraces still defined by stone walls, now overgrown with wild herbs and brambles. Olive trees planted by the former residents continue to fruit, in autumn, unpicked olives litter the ground. A few houses show signs of very basic restoration: new roofs on old walls, padlocked doors, solar panels on one structure. A handful of former residents or their descendants maintain a connection to the village, using the buildings as summer retreats.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.historyP: On 15 April 1979, an earthquake measuring 7.0 on the Richter scale struck the Montenegrin coast. The epicentre was near the coast south of the Riviera, but the damage was widespread. Budva's Old Town suffered significant structural damage, Budva's citadel was heavily hit, and dozens of smaller settlements like Gornji Stoliv were rendered uninhabitable. The earthquake killed 136 people across the region. In Gornji Stoliv, the stone buildings, held together by gravity and friction rather than mortar, cracked and shifted on their foundations. The road was blocked by rockfalls. Residents were evacuated to the waterfront and never returned permanently.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.caution1: Buildings are structurally unsound. Do not enter roofed structures, they could collapse.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.caution2: The access road is narrow and steep. If you meet an oncoming car, one of you must reverse to a passing place.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.caution3: There is no water, no shade, and no phone signal at the village. Bring everything you need.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.caution4: Do not visit after heavy rain, the road surface can wash out on the steepest sections.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.caution5: Snakes are present in the overgrown areas during summer. Stay on the visible paths and wear closed-toe shoes.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.alsoP1: After descending, continue along the Riviera road to the waterfront of Donji Stoliv for a swim from the stone steps directly into the Riviera. Or drive north to Budva and explore the hidden churches we cover in our guide to
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.alsoLink: the secret chapels of Budva's Old Town
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.alsoP2: The drive to Gornji Stoliv pairs well with a visit to the Vrmac ridge trail. The trailhead near Prcanj is only a few minutes from the Stoliv turn-off, and the two experiences, abandoned village and Austro-Hungarian fortress, complement each other perfectly for a half-day of off-the-beaten-path exploration.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.photographyP: Gornji Stoliv is one of the most photogenic locations around the Riviera, but it requires patience. The best light hits the village in the morning, when the sun illuminates the eastern-facing walls and the Riviera below is still in shadow. Wide-angle shots from the upper terraces capture the full sweep of the Riviera with the ruined buildings in the foreground. For detail shots, look for the contrast between the crumbling stonework and the vegetation reclaiming it, fig branches growing through window frames, wildflowers in collapsed doorways, moss on the church bell tower. Late afternoon brings a different mood: long shadows, warm stone colours, and the sound of the evening call to prayer drifting up from the mosque in a settlement across the Riviera.
blogStolivAbandonedVillage.whenToVisitP: Spring and autumn are ideal. Summer mornings work but the heat builds quickly on the exposed hillside, by 11:00 in July, the stone walls radiate heat and there is no shade. Winter visits are possible on dry days, though the access road may be slippery after rain. The village is most atmospheric in the soft light of April or October, when the air is clear and the surrounding vegetation is either blooming or turning golden. Avoid weekends in summer when the occasional tour group from Budva makes the trip.

blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.title: Spring in the Bay: Wildflower Drives from March to May
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.subtitle: The bay bursts into colour between March and May, orchids, iris, and rockrose line the roads from Budva to Risan and up toward the Lovcen foothills.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.description: Spring wildflower driving guide for the Budva Riviera. Where to find orchids, iris, and Mediterranean flora from March to May.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.introP1: Between March and May, the Budva Riviera undergoes a transformation that most visitors miss entirely. The grey karst slopes that dominate winter views explode into colour: wild orchids appear in rocky meadows, purple iris line the Riviera road verges, yellow broom covers entire hillsides, and rockrose (Cistus) paints the lower Vrmac slopes pink and white.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.introP2: Spring is also the quietest season for driving. The cruise ships have not yet arrived, the roads are empty, and the light has the soft clarity that photographers prize. A rental car and a willingness to stop at every layby is all you need.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.introP3: Montenegro's position on the Adriatic coast, combined with the dramatic elevation changes around the Riviera, creates a sequence of bloom periods that extends the wildflower season over nearly three months. Species that flower at sea level in March may not appear until May at 800 metres on the Lovcen slopes. This vertical gradient means that even a short drive uphill from the Riviera road takes you into a different floral zone entirely.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.h2Route1: Route 1: Budva to Risan Bay Road
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route1P1: The 23 km bay road from Budva north through Bečići, Orahovac, and Rafailovići to Risan is the best single-road wildflower drive on the Riviera. The roadside verges burst with iris, red poppies, and wild garlic from late March. Between Rafailovići and Risan, where the road climbs slightly above the water, look for orchid species in the rocky meadows on the inland side. Pull off at any of the unmarked laybys and walk ten metres from the road.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route1P2: Stop at the small layby 2 km past Rafailovići on the left side, where a low stone wall overlooks a grassy slope facing south. In April, this spot is thick with bee orchids, tongue orchids, and early purple orchids growing among the limestone rocks. The orchids are small, you need to look carefully and sometimes kneel to spot them, but once you find the first, you realise they are everywhere. Wild asparagus also grows along this stretch and is foraged by locals for spring omelettes.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.altFlowers: Wild orchids and iris growing beside the Budva Riviera road in spring
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route2P1: Drive the first section of the Lovcen serpentine above Budva, the lower eight to ten hairpin bends, and the vegetation shifts from Mediterranean to sub-alpine. Broom, sage, and rosemary scent the air. In April, the clearings between the switchbacks fill with early-season wildflowers that disappear entirely by June. You do not need to drive the entire serpentine for the floral payoff, the first 5 km above Budva are the most rewarding.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route2P2: At approximately bend 8, a flat grassy area on the outside of the curve supports a particularly dense cluster of spring flowers. Yellow genista (broom) dominates the slopes, interspersed with blue ground ivy and the occasional pale yellow primrose. The air is noticeably cooler here than at sea level, and on a warm April morning, the scent of wild herbs is intense. This spot also offers a dramatic panoramic view of the Riviera below, one of the best photo opportunities combining flowers and landscape.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route3P1: The road from Prcanj up to the Vrmac trailhead passes through abandoned agricultural terraces that have rewilded over the past fifty years. In April and May, these terraces support a dense mix of orchids, anemones, and Mediterranean herbs. Park at the trailhead and walk even a few hundred metres along the ridge path for concentrated spring flora.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.route3P2: The Vrmac ridge itself is one of the richest botanical areas around the Riviera. The sheltered south-facing slopes retain warmth longer than the exposed northern side, creating micro-habitats where heat-loving species (like the Dalmatian iris) grow alongside shade-tolerant orchids. If you continue along the ridge trail, the flora changes with elevation and aspect. The stretch between the trailhead and the Austro-Hungarian fort is particularly rewarding in late April.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species1Val: Several species, including bee orchids and tongue orchids, appear in rocky grassland from April.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species2Val: Purple Dalmatian iris lines roadsides throughout March and April.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species3Val: Pink and white flowers cover the lower hillsides from April through May.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species4Val: Yellow Genista covers entire slopes above 200 metres from late April.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species5Val: Appear in fields and verges from late March, peaking in April.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species6Val: Thin green shoots appear in hedgerows and roadsides from March. Locals gather them for spring omelettes.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.species7Val: Small pink flowers on the forest floor, particularly along the Vrmac ridge trail in shaded sections.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.photographyP: Spring wildflower photography around the Riviera benefits from overcast days, even light without harsh shadows produces the best results. Early morning and late afternoon provide warmer tones. For orchids, get low, shoot from ground level with the Riviera as a background. A macro lens or phone macro mode reveals the extraordinary detail of orchid lip patterns. The best combination shots (flowers with bay landscape) come from the Lovcen foothills, where you can frame wildflowers in the foreground with the Riviera far below.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.tip1Val: Spring days alternate between warm sunshine and sudden rain. Carry a waterproof layer in the car.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.tip2Val: Bay roads are excellent year-round. Mountain roads above 500 metres may still have wet or muddy patches in March.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.tip3Val: Mid-April is the peak bloom for most species around the Riviera. Higher elevations peak later, in May.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.tip4Val: Overcast days produce the best wildflower photographs, even light without harsh shadows. Macro mode on phone cameras works well.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.tip5Val: Spring is shoulder season for car rental in Montenegro. Expect rates 20 to 30 percent lower than July and August, with wider vehicle availability.
blogBudvaSpringWildflowers.alsoP: After the wildflower drives, explore the Bečići waterfront just north of Budva for a coffee stop with bay views. See our guide to

fleetIndex.title: Our Budva Rental Fleet
fleetIndex.subtitle: Seven cars picked for a multi-day Budva base, with day-trips out to Cetinje, Skadar Lake, Durmitor and across the border.
fleetIndex.seoDesc: Seven multi-day rental cars for Budva — mid-size wagons, diesel cruisers and a city Fiat. Specs, fuel range, and which car suits day-trips out of the Riviera.
fleetIndex.intro1: This fleet is built around the multi-day Budva renter — visitors who anchor themselves at a Bečići apartment or an Old Town guesthouse for a fortnight or longer, and who expect to be spending real time on the road between bay-based days. That brief is different from a cruise-day hire or a pure Tivat Airport Airport pickup: it leans on boot space for a weekly shop at Voli, diesel torque for the Sozina motorway run, and a seating position that keeps four adults comfortable on the 3-hour push to Žabljak. The seven cars below are chosen accordingly.
fleetIndex.intro2: The mix is deliberately weighted toward mid-size. A Peugeot 308 with the 1.5 BlueHDi and the EAT auto, a Renault Megane diesel with the segment’s biggest boot, a VW Golf with the DSG 2.0 TDI — these are the cars that turn a Budva → Cetinje → Lake Skadar → Sozina loop from a gear-changing chore into a cruise. For shorter hops we keep a Renault Clio, a Citroen C3 with Advanced Comfort dampers, a Kia Stonic crossover for the rutted spur roads up to Njeguši and Gornja Lastva, and a tiny Fiat 500 for when Muo waterfront parking is the deciding constraint. No one-trick supercars, no vans; every car here justifies a multi-day hire.
fleetIndex.intro3: A practical Budva note before you choose. Diesel remains the smarter spec for anyone planning even one cross-border run — Trebinje is 90 minutes, Dubrovnik via Debeli Brijeg is also 90 minutes, Mostar is a full day each way on a good cruise. The 308 and Megane will cover all three on a single tank, the Golf is the most refined motorway car we have, and the petrol Clio will still do a Budva → Dubrovnik return on half a tank. If your week is bay-contained — Rafailovići, Prčanj, Muo, Risan — the Fiat 500 or the C3 saves a third on weekly fuel and parks in places the rest of the fleet can’t. Pick on your itinerary, not the daily rate.
